title: Breaking a broody hen
subtitle: Getting a hen back into lay when she refuses to move.
excerpt: A broody hen stops laying, monopolises a nest box, pecks at anyone who approaches, and can lose condition if left sitting for weeks on no eggs. Breaking broodiness early protects her health and gets her back into production. The method is straightforward and takes three to five days.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018), gov.uk. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, broodiness management chapters. AHDB Eggs practical guide to laying hen management.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hormonal state in a hen triggered by prolonged nest-sitting, characterised by flat feathering, puffed appearance, growling, refusal to leave the nest, and raised breast temperature. The hen's laying hormone (prolactin) suppresses the egg production hormone (LH).

content.text: is driven by prolactin, the same hormone that suppresses egg production in mammals during nursing. The cure is cooling the broody hen's abdomen and interrupting the sitting pattern. Any method that does both consistently for three to five days will work.

A broody hen sits tight in the nest box, flattens her feathers, growls or pecks when disturbed, and returns to the nest within minutes of being lifted off. Her breast skin is warm to the touch (the broodiness patch runs higher than normal body temperature to incubate eggs). She eats and drinks less than usual and may lose condition if left broody for more than a fortnight.

The most reliable method is a raised wire-mesh cage with a slatted or mesh floor placed somewhere with airflow below it. The hen sits in the cage on wire rather than nesting material; the airflow under the floor cools her abdomen and the absence of a nesting box interrupts the sitting stimulus. She must have water and food in the cage at all times.

Check after three days. If she has calmed down, no longer flattens and growls, and her breast skin has cooled, release her to the flock. If she heads straight back to the nest box, return her to the cage for two more days. Most hens break within three to five days; a heavy-breed hen (Silkie, Orpington) may take seven.

If you do not have a cage, repeatedly lifting the hen off the nest every hour for a full day, removing all nesting material from the box, and blocking off the nest box at night will work for a mildly broody bird. It requires more effort and is less reliable for determined broodiness. Blocking nest boxes at night is a useful preventive step during peak broody season (May to July).

Dunking a broody hen in cold water is sometimes suggested but stresses the bird and does not reliably break broodiness. Ice packs in the nest box cool the wrong surface. Leaving an egg-shaped stone in the nest box encourages sitting rather than breaking it. None of these approaches work as well as a proper broody cage, and the water dipping method causes real welfare cost for minimal gain.
Using broodiness rather than fighting it

If you want to hatch eggs, a broody hen is the most reliable incubator available. Place up to 12 fertile eggs under her the first night after she has been sitting for two full days, confirm them by candling at day 7, and let her run the 21-day hatch without interference beyond daily food and water access. Break her only when she is sitting on no eggs and you do not want chicks.
